mission.title: Install and Configure Redis Operator (Spotahome / OT-CONTAINER-KIT)
mission.description: The Redis Operator by OT-CONTAINER-KIT simplifies the deployment and management of Redis instances on Kubernetes, supporting both standalone and clustered configurations. It is ideal for users looking to automate Redis setups with built-in monitoring and security features. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Redis Operator Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add redis-operator https://opstree.github.io/redis-operator

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Redis Operator repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for the operator to manage Redis resources effectively.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/v0.24.0/deploy/crds/redis.opstree.dev_redisclusters_crd.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/v0.24.0/deploy/crds/redis.opstree.dev_redisfailovers_crd.yaml

```

> Installs the RedisCluster CRD.
> Installs the RedisFailover CRD.

steps.title: Install Redis Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the Redis Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

helm install redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --create-namespace --version 0.24.0

```

> Installs the Redis Operator in the redis-operator namespace.

steps.title: Create a Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a Redis cluster using a custom resource definition.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

```

> Creates a Redis cluster named 'my-redis-cluster' with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

steps.title: Verify Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Redis cluster to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Lists all Redis clusters in the redis-operator namespace.
> Verifies that all Redis pods are running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Redis Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Redis Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all Redis clusters and their data.

```bash

helm uninstall redis-operator --namespace redis-operator

kubectl delete namespace redis-operator

```

> Uninstalls the Redis Operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Redis Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your Redis data. This step upgrades the Redis Operator to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator -o yaml > redis-backup.yaml

helm upgrade redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --version 0.24.0

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Backup current Redis cluster configurations.
> Upgrades the Redis Operator to version 0.24.0.
> Verifies that all Redis pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator -o yaml > redis-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Redis cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRD is not installed correctly or if there are insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Ensure CRDs are installed and check resource availability with 'kubectl describe nodes'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration in the Redis cluster spec.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n redis-operator' for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Redis cluster not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or service misconfigurations may prevent access.

**Fix:**
Verify service configurations and network policies with 'kubectl get svc -n redis-operator'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'RedisFailover not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the RedisFailover CRD is not installed.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the RedisFailover CRD using the provided command in the installation steps.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Redis 7.0, improved monitoring capabilities with redis-exporter.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Redis cluster listed with 'kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator', and all pods in the 'Running' state.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
